observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Treasurer (i) regularly reconcile the cashbook balances with subsidiary accounting records so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and corrected, in compliance with Section 63 of the LTOM; (ii) immediately deposit his collections intact to avoid possible loss or misuse of cash; and (iii) render a satisfactory written explanation on the withholding of collections that led to accumulation of significant balance in the Cash, Local Treasury account; and

OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provisions of the Local Treasury Operations Manual pertaining to the cancellation of stale checks and make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: Due to the non-reconciliation of the Treasury and Accounting records, the audit observation was reiterated in Finding No. 1.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GSO and the Municipal Accountant exert more efforts to reconcile the Report on the Physical Count of PPE, Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards, and other accounting records and any resulting differences must be immediately addressed and adjusted to reflect the correct balance of PPE accounts.
observations.reason: Material discrepancies between the results of the physical count and the accounting records were still noted in the audit of CY 2021 accounts hence, warranting reiteration of the audit observation in Finding No. 5.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Mayor, as agency head, ensure compliance with the guidelines set by the DSWD and other government agencies in the proper documentation of the distribution of similar type financial assistance coursed through the Municipality; and

MSWDO, together with the OIC-Municipal Accountant submit to the Audit Team the lacking SACs which must contain the important details for verification by the Audit Team as well as the other lacking documentary requirements for verification and audit.
observations.reason: The documentary deficiencies are still not submitted to the Audit Team. However, the Municipal Administrator assured that the documents will be submitted for audit.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management, thru the:

BAC and the BAC-TWG: (i) undertake post-qualification proceedings in a meticulous manner in accordance with the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and observe caution in evaluating the documents submitted by bidders during preliminary evaluation and post-qualification before recommending award of contract; (ii) strictly comply with Section 39.1 of the same IRR of R.A. 9184 and ensure that the winning contractors has posted the required performance security before contract signing; and (iii) ensure the posting of the NOA, NTP and approved contracts in the PhilGEPS website in compliance to Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to promote transparency in government procurement; and 

OIC-Municipal Accountant to check the regularity and completeness of supporting documents prescribed under Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing claims for payments and ensure compliance with 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to establish the legality, validity and propriety of the disbursements; and (ii) ensure that the payments of advance payment are supported with a irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by a surety or insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the procuring entity.
observations.reason: The documentary deficiencies are still not submitted to the Audit Team. However, the Municipal Administrator assured that the documents will be submitted for audit.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer regularly reconcile the cashbook balances with subsidiary accounting records in compliance with Section 63 of the LTOM so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and corrected; and transmit to the Accounting Office the DVs and supporting documents for the unrecorded check disbursements appearing in the BRS to facilitate its recording in the books; and
OIC-Municipal Accountant take up in the books the valid book reconciling items duly substantiated with appropriate documentation (e.g. copies of debit and credit memos).

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer (a) update the Real Property Tax Account Registers or Taxpayer’s Index Cards based on the assessment roll submitted by the Municipal Assessor; (b) prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant the certified list of taxpayers showing the current/prior years amount due and collectible at the beginning of every year as basis in setting up the RPT/SET receivables and deferred income accounts; and
Municipal Accountant set up the Real Property Tax Receivable, Special Education Tax Receivable, Deferred Real Property Tax Income and Deferred Special Education Tax Income accounts for CY 2021 and onwards based on the certified list submitted by the Municipal Treasurer showing not only the taxes accruing for the current year but uncollected taxes on prior years as well.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal Accountant, send written notices to other accountable officers requiring settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against the erring accountable officers, if warranted; and (b) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.  We also recommended that the Management adopt the “no liquidation, no cash advance” policy to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances.

We recommended that the GSO and the Municipal Accountant exert more efforts to reconcile the Report on the Physical Count of PPE, Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards, and other accounting records and any resulting differences must be immediately addressed and adjusted to reflect the correct balance of PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
as the head of the agency, ensure that the projects charged against the 20% Development Fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures pursuant to Section 2.3 of the DBM-DILG JMC 2017-1;
thru the BAC (i) ensure that public bidding, as the general mode of procurement, is undertaken for all procurement activities of the LGU, except in highly exceptional cases provided for in the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; (ii) ensure proper application of Section 4.5 (a) of the same IRR and that material assistance given to beneficiaries which involve the purchase of goods are subjected to procurement rules and regulations embodied in RA 9184 and its IRR; and
thru the MPDC or head of implementing office/s, submit to the Audit Team the program/activity design and the guidelines used in selecting the beneficiaries for both the Livestock and Poultry Production and Agricultural Development Program, including the swine dispersal contracts for the Livestock and Poultry Production Program; and
We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant, (i) thru the implementing office/s, submit to the Audit Team the distribution list to substantiate the distribution of swine to support derecognition of Breeding Stocks by ₱2,017,000.00; (ii) ensure timely recording of financial transactions to ensure faithful representation of accounts in the financial statements; and (iii) review and check the completeness of supporting documents as part of the internal audit services of the municipality pursuant to Section 474(b) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and the BAC Secretariat: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents to facilitate technical evaluation and validation of the projects; and (ii) henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 on complete documentation and RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR on the procurement and implementation of infrastructure projects;
Municipal Mayor strictly comply with Section 39.1 of the same IRR of R.A. 9184 and ensure that the winning contractors have posted the required performance security before the contract signing; and
OIC-Municipal Accountant (i) check the regularity and completeness of supporting documents prescribed under Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing claims for payments and ensure compliance with 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to establish the legality, validity and propriety of the disbursements; (ii) ensure that claims for advance payment are duly supported with an irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by a surety or insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the procuring entity; and (iii) ensure that contractors’ requests for the release of retention prior to the end of the defects liability period are substituted with surety bonds callable on demand, of amounts equivalent to the retention money for and acceptable to Government.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor (i) issue termination of contract to erring contractors who have negative slippage of ten percent (10%) or more in the completion of the work pursuant to Section III-A (2) of the Guidelines on Termination of Contracts; (ii) cause the forfeiture of their performance security pursuant to Section 8.5 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer compute and impose liquidated damages to the concerned contractors for the delayed completion of infrastructure projects pursuant to Section 8 of the Contract Implementation Guidelines for the Procurement of Infrastructure Projects.

We recommended that:
Municipal Mayor (i) submit a written explanation as to why the LGU utilized the funds obtained from the CY 2021 LGSF-FA to purchase relief goods instead of Covid-19 vaccines; (ii) ensure that trust funds are utilized only for the purpose for which they were intended; and (iii) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9184; and
OIC-Municipal Accountant (i) check and review the completeness and regularity of supporting documents before processing and certifying the Disbursement Vouchers for payment and accepting liquidation reports; and (ii) submit to the Audit Team the distribution list and RIS to substantiate the receipt of goods.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor ensure that the PO is completely and accurately accomplished before its approval and the complete details regarding the specifications of the goods to be procured are clearly indicated in the PO/Contract.
Municipal Mayor, thru the OIC-Municipal Accountant, BAC and BAC Secretariat, (i) cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents which includes the License to Operate of the supplier of antigen test kits; and (ii) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on common documentary requirements and R.A. No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR on the procurement of goods;
BAC, thru its Chairperson, clarify the mode of procurement used by the LGU and explain the reasons for not conducting public bidding in the procurement of antigen test kits as mandated under R.A. 9184; and
OIC-Municipal Accountant check and review the completeness and regularity of supporting documents before processing and certifying the Disbursement Vouchers for payment.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor, as the head of the agency, ensure that economy and austerity measures are strictly observed in the use of government funds. Instead, focus the utilization of government funds for purposes which are more beneficial to the general public/constituents;
Municipal Mayor, thru the BAC, ensure that public bidding, as the general mode of procurement, is undertaken for all procurement activities of the LGU, except in highly exceptional cases provided for in the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and
OIC-Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer submit to the Audit Team the (i) distribution list duly acknowledged by the recipients of procured items; and (ii) program/activity design for the appropriated amount of ₱3,906,936.00 for Christmas Celebration under Supplemental Budget No. 12 as enacted by Appropriation Ordinance No. 2021-047 dated December 13, 2021.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Department Heads concerned to immediately prepare a consolidated report on all management actions taken relative to the audit observations and recommendations included in the Annual Audit Report for CY 2020 using the prescribed AAPSI Form and henceforth, strictly adhere to the prescribed period for its submission as provided under the GAA.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) (a) through the Municipal Accountant, send written notices to all concerned accountable officers requiring settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if warranted; and (b) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances.
observations.managementAction: The Management sent demand letters to some accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: Significant balances of unliquidated cash advances were still noted in the audit of CY 2021 accounts hence, warranting reiteration of the audit observation in Finding No. 3.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Local Chief Executive, as the agency head, see that the officials who are responsible for the late remittances to the PhilHealth are made personally liable for the surcharges incurred; and

Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant dutifully observe and implement the provisions of Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and the existing laws and regulations on the prompt remittance of amounts due to the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to prevent imposition of charges and penalties and to ensure continued availment of benefits of the employees.
observations.managementAction: Management remitted only what was billed to them. The Municipal Administrator assured the Audit Team that they will secure statement of accounts from the GSIS to facilitate reconciliation between management records and GSIS records.
observations.reason:
Significant balances of inter-agency payables were still noted in the CY 2021 audit.
According to the Management, this was due to disparity between their records. They paid only what was billed by PhilHealth    and Pag-ibig.

observations.managementAction: According to the Municipal Mayor, the Administration, through the supplier, in good faith, had decided to return the amount of ₱93,500 through OR No. 1352645.
observations.reason: The Audit Team still noted incomplete documentation in the disbursement of government funds and special cash advances for the purchase of relief goods in the audit of the CY 2021 accounts and operations of the LGU hence, warranting reiteration of the audit observation in Finding No. 10.

observations.managementAction: The SDO refunded the amount of ₱34,207.00 through OR No. 1342899.
observations.reason: The Audit Team still noted material expenditures incurred in relation to Christmas activities hence, warranting reiteration of the audit observation in Finding No. 12.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: 1. Municipal Accountant ensure the Bank Reconciliation Statements are prepared for each of the eight depository accounts under the Trust Fund; and reconstruct the SLs and reconcile the same with the GL to accurately support its balance of in the financial statements. 2. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer regularly reconcile the cashbooks and accounting records so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and corrected; and strictly comply with Section 59 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I pertaining to the cancellation of stale checks and make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated in Finding No. 1 of this AAR.
observations.reason: No subsidiary ledger for each of the TF bank accounts. The same deficiencies were noted in the audit of the CY 2019 accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: 1. LCE, through the Municipal Accountant, (a) serve written notices to all concerned accountable officers requiring settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if warranted; and (b) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances. 2. Municipal Accountant monitor strictly the grant and liquidation of cash advance to ensure that no additional cash advance is granted unless the previous ones are settled.
observations.managementAction: The Management had constantly reminded the accountable officers to settle/liquidate their outstanding cash advances. Demand letters have already been issued to the accountable officers during the first quarter of CY 2020; however, according to Management, no reply from the concerned individuals have been received. Such being the case, the Audit Team recommended for the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-02 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.reason: The position of the Municipal Accountant was vacant from January to October 2019, hence no written notices were issued to the accountable officers. The year-end unliquidated cash advances of ₱8,011,808.66 showed significant increase by 15.31% or ₱1,063,942.51 from last year’s balance of ₱6,947,866.15. The Due from Officers and Employees account pertains to elected officials and employees who are no longer connected with the LGU and some are already deceased.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE, as the head of the agency: (a) conduct a thorough review and evaluation of all contracts and agreements on projects, programs and activities under the trust fund and ensure the fulfillment of its purpose per conditions stipulated therein, particularly the timely implementation of the programs and projects in order to ensure that the objectives of the fund are met; and (b) regularly monitor the implementation of the projects under the trust fund to avoid cancellation/termination of projects in the future. (Note: the above recommendation appears more related to DILG-RAY but retained here as per table.) We also recommended: 1. LCE ensure that economy and austerity measures are strictly observed in the use of government funds and initiate refund of the cash incentives given without legal basis; 2. Special Disbursing Officer ensure that the liquidation of his cash advances are covered by Report of Disbursements; 3. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant ensure payments are made by check particularly those with material amounts; and 4. Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the disbursements, as part of the internal audit system of the Municipality to determine the validity of the payments and completeness of supporting documents; and record the transactions based on the actual nature of the disbursements.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented. Lacking documentary requirements were noted.
observations.reason: Compliance with procurement and documentation requirements was not observed; the same deficiencies were noted in the audit of CY 2019 accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE, as the head of agency: (a) instruct the concerned officials to immediately submit the lacking supporting documents and a satisfactory written explanation on the herein observations. Henceforth, ensure strict adherence to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, DBM Budget Circular No. 2018-05 and subsequent issuances of the DBM on the grant of CNA incentives; and 2. Members of the Sangguniang Bayan ensure that all changes in the annual budget such as re-appropriation of savings or re-programming of discontinued projects or abandonment of PPAs to any item of expenditure under any class or to any new item of expenditure not included in the annual budget are covered by an appropriation ordinance or supplemental budget and submit the same to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for review.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented.
observations.reason: Lacking documentary requirements were not yet submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE, as the head of agency (a) conduct a thorough review and evaluation of all contracts and agreements on projects, programs and activities under the trust fund and ensure the fulfillment of its purpose per conditions stipulated therein, particularly the timely implementation of the programs and projects in order to ensure that the objectives of the fund are met; and (b) regularly monitor the implementation of the projects under the trust fund to avoid cancellation/termination of projects in the future.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented.
observations.reason: Thus, presented as unimplemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
1. Municipal Treasurer:
a. regularly reconcile the cashbooks with accounting records so that
discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and corrected,
in compliance with Section 63 of the LTOM;
b. immediately deposit his collections intact to avoid possible loss or
misapplication of funds; and
c. render a satisfactory written explanation on the accumulation of
significant balance in the Cash, Local Treasury account.
2. Municipal Accountant reconstruct the SLs and prepare Bank
Reconciliation Statements for each of the ten depository accounts under
the Trust Fund; and
3. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with
Section 59 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I pertaining to the
cancellation of stale checks and make the necessary adjustments in the
books of accounts.

We recommended that the LCE (a) through the Municipal Accountant, serve
written notices to all concerned accountable officers requiring settlement of their
outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if warranted; and
(b) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the
guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of
cash advances to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant (i) thoroughly review all ensuing transactions
concerning cash advances for payroll and payment of salaries, and
henceforth ensure that advances for payroll are equal to the net amount of
payroll for the period; and (ii) ensure the completeness of supporting
documents as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 before any grant
of cash advance for payroll is processedd; and
b. Municipal Treasurer submit a satisfactory written explanation why refund
of cash advances in May 2019 was deposited only in July 2019.

We recommended that the:
1. Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer, exhaust all possible means for the collection of dormant
receivables by sending demand letters to the debtors; and
2. Municipal Accountant (i) strictly comply with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2016-005 on the required analysis of dormant accounts by
performing the regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation
of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors,
accountable officers for strict monitoring and disposition of said accounts;
(ii) prepare the quarterly aging of dormant receivables, unliquidated cash
advances and submit them to the Audit Team; (iii) provide allowance for
impairment of dormant receivables as required under IPSAS 29 for the
fair presentation of receivables and equity account in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the:
a. Inventory Committee (i) complete the physical count of all PPE and ensure
the report’s usability by including all the required information in the
RPCPPE, as illustrated in the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II, and
thereafter reconcile the RPCPPE with the property and accounting
records; and (ii) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road
Network System and submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team;
b. Municipal Accountant (i) maintain PPELC for each class of PPE, RPLC
for each real property and LRLC for local road networks and ensure that
all ledger cards contain complete details/description of the property to
facilitate proper reconciliation with the RPCPPE; (ii) trace/locate the
related documents for the 13 “unaccounted/undetermined” property items
included in the lapsing schedules; (iii) transfer to the appropriate PPE
accounts the costs of seven completed infrastructure projects and
thereafter compute and recognize depreciation expense therefor; (iv)
46
reconcile the variance between the contract costs of five completed
infrastructure projects and their costs per lapsing schedule; and (iv) check
and review related accounting records to ensure that all transactions
pertaining to the construction, acquisition and disposal of PPE are
properly reflected in the books of accounts; and
c. General Services Officer (i) maintain complete property cards with
complete information; and (ii) maintain a local road inventory and road
map and Local Road Network Property Card pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We recommended that the:
1. Local Chief Executive:
a. ensure that job orders only cover piece of work or intermittent job
requiring special or technical skills of short duration not exceeding six
months and that job order agreements/contract of service specifically
state the duties and responsibilities of each contracting party including
the expected outputs or work to be performed;
b. provide a basis for the varying and increasing rates of the JO personnel
including the grant of their wage adjustment, and henceforth, ensure
that allowances or incentives granted are anchored on legal
basis/specific authority of law; and
52
c. ensure judiciousness in the hiring of job order personnel to ensure
equity and eliminate any cause for demoralization and dissension
among local employees. If after careful study, there is a pressing need
to hire additional staff to perform administrative function, consider
hiring casual/contractual employees to be charged against the personal
services, subject to PS cap limitation;
2. Human Resource Management Officer study the present organizational
structure of the Municipality taking into consideration the LGU’s service
requirements and make sure that unnecessary and overlapping functions
with those of the permanent employees are eliminated; and
3. Municipal Accountant (a) ensure that job order payrolls are supported by
duly verified/accepted accomplishment reports and that obligated
expenditures are covered by sufficient appropriations; and (b) observe
consistency in recording transactions in order to ensure faithful
representation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the:
1. Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and the BAC Secretariat: (i)
immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents which are
necessary in the technical evaluation and validation of the projects; and (ii)
henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-
001, RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR on the procurement and
implementation of infrastructure projects;
2. BAC and the BAC-TWG: (i) undertake post-qualification proceedings in
a meticulous manner in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
(ii) observe caution in evaluating the documents submitted by bidders
during preliminary evaluation and post-qualification before
recommending award of contract; (iii) ensure that the bidding documents
clearly and adequately define, among others, the (a) objectives, scope, and
expected outputs and/or results of the proposed contract; (b) eligibility
requirements of Bidders; (c) expected contract duration; and (d)
obligations, duties, and/or functions of the winning bidder and that data
and provisions specific to each procurement and contract are included in
the Bid Data Sheet in compliance to the 5th Edition of the PBDs;
3. LCE (i) issue termination of contract to erring contractors who have
negative slippage of ten percent (10%) or more in the completion of the
work pursuant to Section III-A (2) of the Guidelines on Termination of
66
Contracts; and (ii) cause the forfeiture of their performance security
pursuant to Section 8.5 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
4. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer compute and impose
liquidated damages to the contractors of the 4 infrastructure projects
which were completed beyond contract expiry date pursuant to Section 8
of the Contract Implementation Guidelines for the Procurement of
Infrastructure Projects; and
5. Municipal Accountant ensure that the rules embodied in Annex E of 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 applicable to retention money and its release
are strictly followed.

We recommended that the:
1. LCE, as the head of the agency (i) direct the MPDC and the ME to prioritize
the implementation of all the development projects under the 20% DF to
achieve effectively and efficiently the socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes of the Municipality; (ii) ensure that the projects
charged against the fund partake the nature of investment or capital
expenditures pursuant to Section 2.3 of the DBM-DILG JMC 2017-1; and
(iii) ensure that the PPAs to be funded under the 20% Development Fund
are among the list of allowable projects specified in Section 3.0 of DBM-
DILG JMC 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
2. LCE, thru the Municipal Accountant, BAC and BAC Secretariat, (i) cause
the immediate submission of the lacking documents; and (ii) ensure strict
compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June
14, 2012, R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised IRR;
3. BAC strictly abide by the standards set forth by the R.A. No. 9184, its
Revised IRR and other relevant laws on government procurement; and
4. Municipal Accountant check and review the completeness and regularity
of supporting documents before processing and certifying the
Disbursement Vouchers.

We recommended that the:
1. Mayor, as the head of the agency, ensure that charging of ineligible
expenditures against the LDRRMF is immediately stopped and limit the
charges only to disaster mitigation, prevention, preparedness, response,
rehabilitation and recovery activities;
2. General Services Officer prepare and maintain an inventory list of all
procured and donated items for disaster risk management activities using
formats provided under Annexes P, P-1 and P-2 of COA Circular No. 2014-
002 dated April 15, 2014;
3. Municipal Accountant:
a. take up purchases of relief goods under the appropriate inventory
account and ensure that expenses incurred are recognized only upon
distribution of the relief goods to the intended beneficiaries duly
supported with an RIS;
b. diligently review the disbursements, as part of the Accounting Office’s
internal audit function, to ensure that charges against the LDRRMF
are valid and address disaster risk management activities; and
c. strictly comply with the disclosure requirements provided in Item VII.2
of COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We recommended that the: 1. Inventory Committee (i) immediately conduct the physical inventory of all municipal property; (ii) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System to be reconciled with the Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards, Road Network Ledger Cards and other accounting records; and (iii) submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team. 2. Municipal Accountant (i) submit an updated lapsing schedule showing the date of acquisition, acquisition cost, estimated useful life, residual value, monthly depreciation and accumulated depreciation of each item of the PPE; (ii) maintain PPELC for each class of PPE account, RPLC for each real property and LRLC for road networks and ensure that all ledger cards contain complete details/description of the property to facilitate proper reconciliation with the RPCPPE; (iii) submit to this Office the proof of ownership or rights of the Municipal Government over the land used as basis for recording in the books of accounts; (iv) trace/locate the related documents for the “unaccounted” properties included in the lapsing schedule for unidentified properties to substantiate the balances of the PPE balances; and (v) identify if the items of inventory recorded as PPE had already been issued in prior years and thereafter effect the necessary adjustments. Unissued items shall be recognized as inventory. If all items had been issued, close the affected PPE account and its corresponding accumulated depreciation and recognize a Prior Period Adjustment for the difference. 3. Municipal Treasurer (i) maintain complete property cards with complete information to be reconciled with Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE) and accounting records; and (ii) maintain a local road inventory and road map and LRNPC pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.
observations.managementAction: Incomplete PPE was partially counted. The Accounting Office had already started maintaining PPE Ledger Cards. The General Services Office personnel were informed to maintain the records required.
observations.reason: Physical count of PPE was conducted and a partial inventory report was submitted during the year. The same deficiencies were noted in the audit of the CY 2019 accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: 1. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure timely and full remittance of all taxes withheld by the Municipal Government to the BIR to avoid penalties and legal charges; and 2. Municipal Accountant prepare a schedule identifying the portion of tax remittances made during the year applicable to the GF, SEF and TF and effect the necessary adjustments, as appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Due to BIR account was noted as of year-end.
observations.reason: A substantial balance of ₱1,546,679.11 of Due to BIR account was noted as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: 1. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant observe and implement the provisions of Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and the existing laws and regulations on remittances of amounts due to the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG; remit promptly all the collections due to the concerned government agencies to prevent imposition of charges and penalties and to ensure continued availment of benefits of the employees. Any surcharges and penalties incurred due to late remittances shall not be charged against the government fund but to be shouldered and paid from personal fund of the officers responsible for the late remittances. 2. Municipal Accountant maintain the GL and SLs for the Due to GSIS and Due to PhilHealth accounts; and effect the necessary corrections to properly reflect the balance of the Due to PhilHealth account.
observations.managementAction: Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to PhilHealth and Due to PAG-IBIG accounts amounting to ₱1,122,249.88, ₱376,758.31 and ₱102,999.14, respectively, were noted as of year-end.
observations.reason: A substantial increase in the balances of the Due to GSIS, Due to PhilHealth and Due to PAG-IBIG accounts amounting to ₱1,122,249.88, ₱376,758.31 and ₱102,999.14, respectively, were noted as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the required reports and documents in such form and within the period prescribed in the NGAS Manual for LGUs and COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.
observations.managementAction: Financial reports were submitted earlier this year on February 27, 2020.

MDRRMO designate observe the regular monthly submission of the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF duly certified correct by the Local Accountant to the COA Audit Team. 4. Municipal Accountant: (a) effect the necessary adjusting entries for the actual unissued inventory items on hand, if any; (b) ensure the proper recording of small items purchased for disaster and rescue activities by debiting the appropriate inventory account upon purchase and recognize an expense only when the items are actually issued; and (c) diligently review the disbursements, as part of the Accounting Office's internal audit function, to ensure that each claim is duly supported with proper documentary requirements. 6. Members of the Bids and Awards Committee (a) strictly adhere to the provisions of the procurement law; and (b) submit justification for the above noted deficiencies; and 7. reconcile the same with the GL to accurately support its balance of in

corrected; and
26
b. strictly comply with Section 59 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs,
Volume I pertaining to the cancellation of stale checks and make the

We recommended that the:

We recommended that the:
a. Inventory Committee (i) immediately conduct the physical inventory of all
municipal property; (ii) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE
and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System to be
reconciled with the Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards, Road Network
Ledger Cards and other accounting records; and (iii) submit a copy thereof

b. Municipal Accountant (i) submit an updated lapsing schedule showing the
date of acquisition, acquisition cost, estimated useful life, residual value,
monthly depreciation and accumulated depreciation of each item of the PPE;
(ii) maintain PPELC for each class of PPE account, RPLC for each real
property and LRLC for road networks and ensure that all ledger cards
contain complete details/description of the property to facilitate proper

ownership or rights of the Municipal Government over the land used as basis

documents for the “unaccounted” properties included in the lapsing schedule
for unidentified properties to substantiate the balances of the PPE balances;
and (v) identify if the items of inventory recorded as PPE had already been

observations.recommendation:
We recommended strict compliance with the provisions prescribed by Sec. 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and LBC No. 98 dated October 14, 2011. The Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Finance Committee should perform a thorough review and evaluation of the budget, whether annual or supplemental, prior to its submission to the Sangguniang Bayan to ensure that all PS items are properly identified and classified as PS and that appropriations for PS items shall not exceed the PS limitation at any point in time during the budget year.
To be able to implement and provide additional PS costs, the municipal government should make an effort in raising revenues/receipts or reducing its PS expenses. If it is inevitable to hire additional manpower, provide appropriations for salaries and wages of casual and job order employees under Personal Services. Otherwise, maximize the service of existing employees and minimize the hiring of casual and job order employees.
The Municipal Accountant should properly record the wages of JO/personnel performing administrative, clerical to proper personal services accounts in order to correctly present the actual PS expenditures of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Computation of PS Expenditures over PS Limitation based on CY 2016 Total Income from Regular Sources (GF) and CY 2018 actual PS expenditures revealed that the LGU did not exceed the PS limitation for CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended for the management to ensure that: a. Job orders only cover piece of work or intermittent job requiring special or technical skills of short duration not exceeding six months; b. Job order payrolls are supported by duly verified accomplishment reports; c. wages of personnel performing administrative, clerical and utility works are recorded to proper Personal Services accounts as prescribed in COA Circular 2015-009; and d. wages of personnel which are PS in nature should not be charged against the appropriations for MOOE; e. In addition, the Management should provide a basis or justification for the varying and increasing rates of the job order employees. The management should also study the present organizational structure of the Municipality taking into consideration the LGU’s service requirements and make sure that unnecessary and overlapping functions with those of the permanent employees are eliminated. Also, judiciousness in the hiring of casual, contractual and job order personnel should be observed, taking into account the austerity measures prescribed by the government. Lastly, the Municipality should immediately stop the practice of utilizing the 20% Development Fund for the payment of personal services expenditures and adhere strictly with the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should require the Local Development Council during the preparation of the annual budget and the 20% DF to include only PPAs which are feasible, specific and properly identified as to target date of execution and the personnel in-charge responsible for the implementation. Likewise, coordinate with the Municipal Engineering so that the same shall be supported with POWs to ensure optimum utilization of the funds and timely and efficient implementation  of the projects.

Present to the Local Finance Committee the unimplemented PPAs and the balances of continuing appropriations every end of the year for further review and for possible re-programming and inclusion in the annual budget preparation. Unexpended balances of appropriations of projects/programs/activities which were already completed/accomplished should be re-appropriated for other programs/projects/activities for the ensuing year.
Lastly, the Municipal Planning and Development  Coordinator together with the Local Development Council should coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development programs, projects and activities of the Municipality in accordance with the approved development plan and properly render a report to the Mayor the status of every project, problems encountered and of any recommended measures to address such problem.
observations.reason: Various PPAs under the LGU’s 20% DF still remained unimplemented as of year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
We reiterate that the inventory committee should check the condition of the property, any damage, obsolete and unserviceable property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable and Waste Material Report and immediately request for disposal from the Commission.
Require each department head to fast track inventory of all properties under their office and submit the inventory report to the committee for validation and verification of the correctness and existence of the property.
The inventory committee to fully accomplish the RPCPPE, indicating the actual count and complete details, and the results of the actual count shall be reconciled with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately.  From thereon, perform periodic inventory of PPE and prepare report on the results thereof, in accordance with existing regulations.
The LCE should require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain ledger cards for each class and category of  property and equipment.  Work back on the previous years, based on the delivery receipt, acceptance and inspection report and other source documents prepare property ledger card indicating acquisition date, cost and the brief descriptions which serves as the subsidiary ledgers of fixed assets per classs and category.  For the purpose of reconciling the accounting records,  the reported balance of PPE per Balance Sheet is the based amount of property per category.  Based on ledger cards prepare lapsing schedule for the computation and monitoring of depreciation charges.
The Treasurer Office to maintain Property Cards per class and category of property, plant and equipment indicating acquisition date, the brief descriptions and work back on the previous years and reconcile with the records of the accounting office to determine the correctness of the property records.  Update the property issue slip/PAR to check custodianship of the property.

We recommended that the management should send Demand Letters to those officials/employees separated from the service to immediately liquidate/settle their cash advances.
We also recommended that the management should impose salary deductions from those officials and employees who have not settled their cash advances for travel or for other purposes, as well as the institution of other sanctions, as provided for under Sections 5.1.3 and 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 16 of Executive Order 248.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should (a) identify and reconstruct the records of dormant account; (b) reconcile these records against that of the concerned debtor, creditors, national agencies and LGUs; (c) enforce collection by sending demand and confirmation letters. If the analysis is not possible due to the absence of records and documents, request for write-off of these accounts balances from the Commission on Audit pursuant to 2009 Revised Rules of Procedures together with the approved resolution by the Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended strict compliance with the provisions prescribed by Sec. 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and LBC No. 98 dated October 14, 2011. The Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Finance Committee should perform a thorough review and evaluation of the budget, whether annual or supplemental, prior to its submission to the Sangguniang Bayan to ensure that all PS items are properly identified and classified as PS and that appropriations for PS items shall not exceed the PS limitation at any point in time during the budget year.
To be able to implement and provide additional PS costs, the municipal government should make an effort in raising revenues/receipts or reducing its PS expenses. If it is inevitable to hire additional manpower, provide appropriations for salaries and wages of casual and job order employees under Personal Services. Otherwise, maximize the service of existing employees and minimize the hiring of casual and job order employees.
The Municipal Accountant should properly record the wages of JO/personnel performing administrative, clerical to proper personal services accounts in order to correctly present the actual PS expenditures of the municipality.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to submit certification/justifications of the amounts claimed in excess of the authorized travel allowances and transportation expenses; 1) The Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the disbursement or liquidation vouchers to determine completeness of supporting documents, compliance of claims with applicable laws, rules and regulations on travel and correctness of computations, before recording the same in the books of accounts; 2) ensure that the provisions of Executive Order Nos. 248, 248-A and 298, COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012 are strictly complied with; and 3) travelling expenses are recorded to the Travelling Expense account.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to require the Municipal Health Officer or requisitioning officer to verify and certify that the products being requisitioned fall within and conform with the latest edition of the PNF Manual as required in the A.O. No. 163.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017 – 1 dated February 22, 2017 are faithfully complied with and that all the programs, projects and activities chargeable agains the 20% Development Fund in order to attain the intended purpose of the said fund.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile and submit the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should require the Local Development Council during the preparation of the annual budget and the 20% DF to include only PPAs which are feasible, specific and properly identified as to target date of execution and the personnel in-charge responsible for the implementation. Likewise, coordinate with the Municipal Engineering so that the same shall be supported with POWs to ensure optimum utilization of the funds and timely and efficient implementation  of the projects.

Present to the Local Finance Committee the unimplemented PPAs and the balances of continuing appropriations every end of the year for further review and for possible re-programming and inclusion in the annual budget preparation. Unexpended balances of appropriations of projects/programs/activities which were already completed/accomplished should be re-appropriated for other programs/projects/activities for the ensuing year.
Lastly, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator together with the Local Development Council should coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development programs, projects and activities of the Municipality in accordance with the approved development plan and properly render a report to the Mayor the status of every project, problems encountered and of any recommended measures to address such problem.

We recommended that the LDRRM Officer submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to instruct the responsible officials to fast track the implementation of the unimplemented projects and liquidate the funds for the fully implemented projects pursuant COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant should exert prodigious effort to identify the individual balances of the projects recorded under the Due to NGAs account with no available supporting documents and subsidiary schedules and return to the Source Agencies (SA) any unused or unutilized balance for the fully implemented projects. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant must keep separate subsidiary records for the trust liabilities whether or not a separate bank account is maintained.
We recommended that the LGU provide for the 1% LCPC funding allocation in their Annual Budget in the succeeding years.
We recommended that management should prioritize the formulation of MSWMP and comply with the requirements and provisions set forth in RA 9003 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations.
observations.managementAction: In its letter dated January 9, 2018, management commented that the LGU has started drafting the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan. Furthermore, an allocation of P3,000,000 has been provided in 2017 for the initial groundworks and documentation of the sanitary landfill.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality should be more prudent in the utilization of its LDRRMF, taking into consideration the existing rules and regulations governing the utilization of the said fund.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to instruct the Municipal Engineer to submit the list of on-going government PPAs and those to be implemented during the year.  The LCE should regularly submit the monitoring report and to submit at the beginning of the year a list of on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor should create an Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of PPE and submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs, Volume I. He should likewise ensure that physical inventory be undertaken annually and reports be submitted accordingly. Failure to do is indicative of management’s lack of appreciation on the importance of conducting physical inventory.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC, TWG and GSO to render explanation/justification on the above noted deficiencies and violations. Erring officials and employees who may be found guilty may be held criminally and administratively liable under CSC rules and other pertinent laws, rules and regulations.

The Municipality should strictly abide by the standards set forth by R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised IRR and other relevant laws on government procurement. The BAC should exercise due diligence on the review and evaluation of the legality of the alternative mode of procurement before resorting to shopping, small value procurement or negotiated procurement. They should likewise conduct rigid screening of eligibility of suppliers/contractors and regularly update the compilation of its shortlist.

We recommended for the responsible persons in the approval of the said transaction to render an explanation/justification on the above-noted violations. Erring officials and employees who may be found guilty may be held criminally and administratively liable under CSC rules and other pertinent laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipality should ensure that the disbursements to be charged under the current year’s LDRRMF and unexpended balances of previous years are aligned with the approved annual LDRRMP/LDRRMFIP.

The Municipality should also ensure that a comprehensive and integrated plan be prepared annually in accordance with the national, regional and provincial framework and policies on the disaster risk reduction and in close coordination with the LDRRMC. The plan should cover projects and activities to be funded by current year’s LDRRMF, as well as the unexpended balances of previous years upon recommendation of the LDRRMC.

Henceforth, strict compliance with the provisions of the aforementioned regulations on the allocation, utilization and management of the LDRRMF is hereby enjoined.

We recommended that the Local Development Council and the Municipal Planning and Development Council should ensure that the PPAs appropriated under the 20% Development Fund are in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 and supported with identified, detailed and specific programs and projects in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP).

The Local Chief Executive and the Budget Officer should likewise ensure that the fund utilization is restricted to those priority development projects identified under the said regulations.

Management should also follow up from the MLGOO and/or the DILG Regional Office for the review action made on the submitted GPB and ensure that it is appropriately evaluated to guarantee that the proposed PPAs are part of the GAD priority agenda of the government and that activities are sufficiently funded.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Development Council during the preparation of the annual budget for the 20% Development Fund to include only PPAs which are feasible, specific and properly identified as to target date of execution and the personnel in-charge responsible for the implementation and coordinate with the Municipal Engineering so that the same shall be supported with Program of Works to ensure optimum fund utilization and timely and efficient project implementation.

The Local Finance Committee should review the balances of continuing appropriations for every end of the year for possible inclusion in the preparation of the annual budget. Unexpended balances of appropriations for PPAs which were already completed/accomplished should be re-appropriated for other projects of the ensuing year.

Lastly, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, together with the Local Development Council, should coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development PPAs of the Municipality in accordance with the approved development plan and properly render a report to the Municipal Mayor the status of every project, problems encountered and recommendations to address such problem.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that unexpended 20% Development Fund were already re-programmed and the implementation of development projects is the priority of the current administration.

observations.managementAction: No bonuses were granted during the year except those allowed under RA6758.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should properly record the payment of monetization of leave credits under the account “Other Personnel Benefits”, in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the HRMO should strictly review the balance of leave credits of each employee/official and refrain from certifying the application for leave monetization of officials and employees who have less than fifteen (15) days of accumulated vacation leave credits. Similarly, the Municipal Accountant should further review and check the approved application before processing the said transactions.

All concerned officials are also enjoined to strictly follow the provisions of the Omnibus Rules on Leave and other pertinent CSC rules and regulations on the monetization of leave credits.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the budget for the ensuing years are prepared in accordance with the procedures, schedules and local budget preparation forms prescribed under the Budget Operations Manual for LGUs and for the Sangguniang Bayan to require, prior to the approval of the Annual and Supplemental Budgets, that special purpose appropriations are categorized by object of expenditure and presented in a similar sequence as a department or office expenditure program except the staffing and compensation profile where there are no personnel and office to operate.

We recommended that the Municipality should strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 10121 on the management and use of the LDRRMF, particularly on the appropriation of the fund which is not less than five percent (5%) of the estimated revenue from regular sources. Management should also immediately transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE for CY 2015 to a special trust fund under account “Trust Liabilities-DRRM” in the trust fund books pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 and Section 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 to ensure that funds are available for disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC in times of calamities. It would also ensure that the unexpended balance is properly recorded and accounted to avoid possible misuse.

We recommended that Management and the members of the GAD Focal Point System should strictly adhere with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA IMC No. 2013-01 on the budget allocation and on the preparation of GPB and AR and submission thereof to the concerned agencies.

Lastly, proper training and seminar should be intensified to the members of the GAD Focal Point System relative to GAD concepts, principles and policies, especially on GAD planning and budgeting to enhance and broaden their knowledge and proper execution of GAD PPAs.
